title: Dyeing with weld for yellow
subtitle: The most lightfast yellow from a British native dye plant
excerpt: Weld (Reseda luteola) gives the most lightfast yellow of any European dye plant. It grows as a biennial on well-drained alkaline soils and can be grown in a kitchen garden. The whole above-ground plant is used in the dye bath. On alum-mordanted wool it gives a clear, warm yellow with good wash fastness.
sourceNotes: Weld as a yellow dye is documented from Roman times onward in Britain. Its superior lightfastness compared with other yellow dye plants is consistent across natural dyeing texts. Growing notes from RHS guidance on Reseda luteola. Observation-led.

content.text: native to chalk and limestone grasslands across Britain and Europe. Its pigment,

yellow dye plant available in Britain. It can be grown in a well-drained, slightly alkaline garden bed: sow thinly in spring and harvest the above-ground plant in the second year when it comes into flower. The whole fresh or dried plant above the root is used. If growing weld is not possible, it is available dried from natural dyeing suppliers.

attrs.body: Wear dedicated gloves, work in a ventilated space, and keep mordant jars away from food and children.
attrs.title: Mordant safety

items.name: Alum-mordanted wool yarn or fleece, pre-wetted
Weld plant material (fresh stems and flowers, or dried)
Stainless steel dye pot, minimum 4 litre

content.text: Place the weld plant material in the dye pot (fresh material can go in whole; dried material should be soaked in cold water for 1 hour first). Cover with water and bring slowly to 80°C over 30 minutes. Simmer at 80°C for 45 minutes. Strain out the spent plant material. The dye bath should be a rich golden-amber colour.

Allow the dye bath to cool to 60°C. Enter the pre-wetted, mordanted fibre. Raise to 80-85°C slowly over 30 minutes, stirring gently every 5 minutes. Maintain for 45-60 minutes. The colour deepens as the luteolin bonds to the alum-mordanted fibre.

Cool to 40°C, then rinse the fibre in warm water until the rinse runs clear. Dry in shade. The finished yellow is stable to light and washing. It can be overdyed with an indigo vat to produce a clear green, or modified with an iron afterbath to produce olive.

items.fix: Harvest weld when the first flowers open on the spike but before they set seed. This is the peak of luteolin concentration. Fresh, correctly timed weld gives a much stronger colour than overripe or stored material.
items.cause: The weld was harvested before flowering (lower luteolin content) or the plant material was old and degraded.
items.symptom: Yellow is pale despite using plenty of plant material

items.fix: Ensure the water used is not hard alkaline tap water. A small addition of white vinegar (enough to bring pH to 5-6, measured with pH strips) corrects a greenish shift and restores clear yellow.
items.cause: The dye bath pH is too alkaline, which shifts luteolin yellow toward green.
items.symptom: Colour shifts toward green on the yarn
